text.text:
I had one of the most delightful dinners at Osteria 16. We didn’t have a reservation, but the staff went out of their way to accommodate us. We were seated at a small counter in the back, which turned out to be extremely comfortable, with the staff giving us attentive service throughout.

The restaurant offers a fixed-price set menu with several small plates. The portions are well-balanced, so you leave feeling satisfied but not overly full. They also have a great wine list—I paired my meal with an organic orange wine, which was fantastic. One small suggestion: it would be great if they offered pairing recommendations based on the menu.

Emil, the owner, was incredibly warm and welcoming, always engaging with guests, explaining the dishes, and sharing great conversations. The restaurant is small but has a great atmosphere, with the kitchen centrality-right positioned, adding to the lively flow of the space.

If I had one minor critique, the heating was a bit too warm, and after a few glasses of wine, I found myself needing to step outside to cool down. Slightly lowering the temperature would make the experience even more comfortable.

Overall, Osteria 16 is a fantastic spot, conveniently located near the underground. Highly recommend if you’re in Copenhagen!

Even if you choose the grill platters or skewers you get a whole flat bread to go with it. I tend to prefer the grill or skewers over the plain pita bread or durum which you can get anywhere else anyway.

The service is always kind, friendly and accommodating.

It's ideal both for dine-in and for takeout and there's also some parking in the side street just opposite the road.

GAO Dumpling Bar is a solid spot if you’re craving dumplings in Copenhagen. The dumplings were full of flavor and came swimming in a rich, spicy chili oil sauce that added a real kick – especially the ones topped with cucumber and black sesame. The texture was spot on: nicely steamed and juicy inside.

We sat outside on the little street patio, which had a very relaxed, casual vibe. It’s not fancy, but it works. The street is full of graffiti and character, and it all adds to the charm in its own way. We had a bottle of San Pellegrino and some extra soy sauce on the side – simple but satisfying.

Service was quick, and the food came out hot. The portion size was decent, though a few more dumplings wouldn’t hurt for the price. Also, seating is limited, so expect to squeeze in or get takeaway if it’s busy.

All in all, GAO is definitely worth a visit for a tasty, spicy dumpling fix – just don’t expect fine dining. We’ll be back!

text.text:
A Slice of Rome in the Heart of Copenhagen
I recently had the pleasure of dining at a remarkable Roman-style pizzeria in Copenhagen that transported me straight to the bustling streets of Trastevere. From the moment I stepped inside, the warm and inviting atmosphere set the stage for what was to be an unforgettable culinary journey.
The star of the show was undoubtedly the pizza. True to its Roman origins, the crust was impeccably thin and delightfully crispy, providing the perfect canvas for the high-quality, flavorful toppings. Each bite was a testament to the skill and passion of the pizzaiolo.
But the excellence did not stop there. The antipasti were a revelation, a vibrant and delicious prelude to the main event. Fresh, authentic ingredients were expertly combined to create a selection of starters that were both traditional and exciting.
What truly elevated the experience, however, was the exceptional wine tasting journey. The curated selection of wines was impressive, and the knowledgeable staff guided me through a superb pairing that enhanced every dish. Each glass was a discovery, perfectly complementing the flavors of the food and telling a story of its own.
This pizzeria is more than just a place to eat; it is an experience. The combination of outstanding Roman-style pizza, delectable antipasti, and a masterfully curated wine selection, all enjoyed in a cozy and welcoming setting, makes it a must-visit destination for any food and wine lover in Copenhagen. I cannot recommend it highly enough.

text.text:
I’ve eaten in many places around the world, and this restaurant stands out for something that can’t be limited to just a gastronomic experience!

The atmosphere, the energy, the way you’re met as a human being. Me and my family just felt so welcome in such a profound humane way, if it makes sense.

For us it felt being in the center of Berlin where you have a feeling of being "free" not limited to gender, identity or some kind of a "stereotype" version to fit in.

I don't think the majority of Danish people know about DEI - Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion - and Stempel just nailed it with their way of being.

Such a beautiful and chilled place, amazing dinner in surroundings which felt like an all inclusive experience.

Stempel Bar & Kitchen sits right by Enghave Plads Metro Station, so close that arriving feels effortless, and so near the center of Copenhagen

Enghave Plads itself is a beautiful open square, social, with seating, greenery, and a small fountain that gives the area a gentle pulse for everyone regardless of who you are.

When my family and I walked in, we were welcomed with warmth that stayed with us the entire visit.

Our server’s presence felt attentive without being intrusive—calm, kind, and genuinely caring. Even as we left, we were sent off with gentle warm smile and a “Merry Christmas”.

The food matched that feeling—beautifully prepared and genuinely satisfying. If you’re local or just passing through, Stempel is a strong choice for brunch, lunch, and dinner as well.

--They describe themselves as a cozy, spacious place with room for everyone, and that rang true for us--

💞💞 Thank you Stempel 💞💞

text.text: From the traditional to the slightly unusual this place has really good pizza. I went with my children and some friends and it was so delicious we went back a second time. I was really happy to see that they had children portions since my youngest one never finishes anything. There's vegetarian options as well as gluten free and there's also options with lamb. I had the lamb with potatoes and it was delicious as was their simple Margarita with pepperoni. This is a strictly to go place but there's tables outside across the street which most people sit at. The only downside to this for me was the waste of pizza containers for every pizza ordered. They serve soft drinks as well as craft beer in bottles and cans. I was surprised to see Westmalle Triple, my favorite beer. Ych has no bathroom, but the bathroom is available at the cafe next door. This is definitely one of the better pizza joints in Copenhagen really good pizza at an affordable price in a cool neighborhood.

text.text:
4 stars—because I love hot dogs. But let’s be honest: it’s not Iceland.

Look, I appreciate what Copenhagen Hot Dog Deli is doing. They’ve got options, they’ve got variety, they’re working hard to impress. But here’s the thing: they’re trying to beat Iceland, and it shows. Desperately.

First off—pork? Come on. Iceland uses lamb. You can taste the difference. There’s just no competing with that level of richness and tradition. Danish dogs, while fine, feel more mass-produced—probably because Denmark’s a bigger country with more mouths to feed.

They offer lots of topping combos, sure—but it feels like they’re throwing ideas at the wall hoping something will match the magic. My best advice? Put the toppings under the hot dog. Iceland figured that out years ago. Danish dogs spill everywhere like they were designed by someone who’s never eaten one in a moving crowd.

For a city that prides itself on sustainability, the amount of wasted pickles, onions, and crispy bits hitting the pavement is… tragic.

That said, it’s still a hot dog—and I do love hot dogs.
So: 4 stars. Just not the fifth. That one lives in Reykjavik.

text.text:
I recently visited Bevi Bevi in Copenhagen with a group of friends (we were 7 in total), and while the overall experience was enjoyable, I have to admit I was a bit underwhelmed by the set menu this time.

The concept of the restaurant is a fixed menu with several courses brought to the table throughout the evening. The service was incredibly kind and attentive, and we felt very comfortable and at ease during the whole dinner — which made for a great atmosphere.

That said, the food itself felt a bit too simple and not very filling. We started with a fennel-like salad with just a couple of orange slices and some olives. We also had sliced fresh tomatoes with olives, which again felt quite basic. The prosciutto with melon was nice but very classic, and the portion of cannelloni (just one per person) was quite small.

The highlights for me were the burrata with cherry tomatoes, creamy and flavorful, and the vitello tonnato, which was well-executed. Still, compared to past visits my friends had, the menu this time felt less creative and a bit disappointing.
In the end, Bevi Bevi feels more like a place to go for drinks with friends, where you can have a few small bites on the side, not necessarily a spot you’d go to for a meal focused solely on food. Hopefully next time the menu will feel more inspired.

We recently had a lovely dinner at this charming little Italian restaurant. The staff were incredibly friendly, and the cozy atmosphere made for a delightful experience. We chose the set menu and added a side of focaccia with olive oil. The olive oil was delicious, though the focaccia was slightly dry.

The set menu included tasty shared dishes like ceviche, charcuterie, and ravioli, followed by a perfectly cooked steak. The only downside was the side salad, which was heavy on the stalks and felt a bit unbalanced. However, the tiramisu was the perfect sweet finish, and we would happily return to try some of the other dishes!
